name: Atlaua
description: Atlaua, in Aztec mythology, was a water deity, specifically associated with fishermen and archers. He was revered as a protector of those who navigated and relied on the waters for sustenance and livelihood. Atlaua's name translates to 'Spear-Thrower' or 'Water Thrower,' reflecting his dominion over the aquatic realm. He was believed to possess the power to control the currents, depths, and inhabitants of lakes, rivers, and the sea. Fishermen would offer him prayers and sacrifices to ensure bountiful catches and safe journeys. Archers, too, sought his favor, perhaps due to the association of spears and projectiles with water. While not as prominent as some other Aztec deities like Tlaloc or Chalchiuhtlicue, Atlaua held a significant position in the lives of those intimately connected with water. His influence extended to navigation and the prosperity derived from aquatic resources. He was often depicted with aquatic symbols and colors, embodying the vital role of water in Aztec society. Evidence of his worship is found in archaeological remains near bodies of water, indicating dedicated sites for offerings and rituals. Atlaua's existence highlights the Aztec's deep understanding of and dependence on the natural world and the importance of respecting the forces that governed it. He was a crucial figure in ensuring the well-being and success of communities reliant on water-based activities, symbolizing the power and unpredictability of the aquatic domain.
subtitle: God of Water, Fishermen, and Archers

keyMyths.title: The Bountiful Catch
keyMyths.description: Fishermen prayed to Atlaua for a large catch. Through their devotion, Atlaua guided them to a sea brimming with fish.

keyMyths.title: The Archer's Aim
keyMyths.description: An archer sought Atlaua's blessing before a crucial hunt. Atlaua granted him perfect aim, ensuring a successful hunt for his people.

keyMyths.title: Safe Passage
keyMyths.description: Sailors beseeched Atlaua for protection during a storm. Atlaua calmed the waters and guided them safely to shore.
